posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
1962 Chateau Des Jacques, Moulin-A-Vent, Beaujolais. Oh my God. This was absolutely STUNNING!! The color looked excellent through the glass so I had fairly high expectations, but as soon as I pulled the cork (Durand cork for sure!), the scents of red berries and sweet Earth leapt from the bottle and I knew that I was in for a wild ride. In the glass, the color is even prettier than through the bottle. I had to take a pic outside to try and capture it (along with a really great AZ sunset). In the mouth, this truly stopped me in my tracks. It explodes with sweet red fruit and hints of cinnamon, then slowly progresses to rose and fall leaves on the mid-palate, and ends with an incredibly long finish filled with minerality and waves of acidity. I truly have no words here - one of the better bottles of wine that I can recall drinking in a long, long time. A stunning and completely etheral experience. @William_Kelley
Would dig this the most!

https://www.instagram.com/p/C2oBzZ-JOUZ/?igsh=MW5uYndtMmN2bWRteQ==

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
I had the 1949 Thorin Chateau des Jacques in the late 1980s - and it was really stunning. I remember it being almost black in color, and really fat in texture. We were opening quite a few Premier Cru Burgundies from the late 1940s/early 1950s that night - and that Moulin A Vent stood right up to them -

Great note Rich!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What’s more it’s not just regular Moulin-à-Vent, but from the Clos de Grand Carquelin, one of the top terroirs of Moulin-à-Vent, which you can see here: Google Maps

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
I love seeing the note and pictures on such a cool wine! The color looks fantastic. I imagine it couldn’t have been in better condition. I’m a huge fan of 30-35 year old Beaujolais, but I haven’t had anything close to this old (like a couple of other people mentioned).

Another nod here to mature Beaujolais sometimes being as good as some much more prestigious wines from Burgundy (not the really great ones, but still…). It makes me wonder what the wines from the '80s and before tasted like on release, and how some of the modern wines will compare in a couple of decades.

posts.name: Emery Adoradio
posts.cooked: Question for all: do you think this longevity is only applicable to Moulin-a-Vent?

I d say to Moulin a Vent and Morgon, but especially the Chateau de Jacques/Thorin, not those which are made in the usual fruity cabonique fermented style (Duboeuf) for drinking quite young. I ve had 2-3 older ones from other producers, Morgon and Moulin aV, but only 25+ y old, that were fine, but e.g. Fleury or St.Amour didn t stay that well.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked: @CFu went through a ‘50s Bojo thing awhile back … maybe he will chime in

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Duboeuf these days is more thermovinification than carbo in the strict sense.

What’s more 1962 is before Jadot purchased the Ch. de Jaques and introduced a more “Côte d’Orien” winemaking style with destemming, long macerations and new oak. Over the last decade, Ch de Jacques has pulled back from the extremes, making more supple wines with a much more discreet élevage signature, something for which we can thank Cyril Chirouze who is now making the wines at Clos Rougeard (as of 2023 vintage).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The prerequisites for that are ripe grapes and winemaking/maturation/bottling practices adapted to producing long-lived wines.

In theory, these could be replicated in most places in the Beaujolais crus, especially today where you can even get good ripeness in e.g. Chiroubles; but for historical reasons the best terroirs of Moulin-a-Vent (under which label the best wines of Fleurie and Chénas were often sold) have tended to be the ones that have been handled that way by their producers. That can be a double-edged sword, as MaV is sometimes the victim of “aspirational” winemaking with clumsy extraction and in-your-face oak, something that’s more seldom inflicted on less prestigious crus.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
Tom_Blach:

Thorin was apparently a very wealthy negociant and consistently used corks of the very highest quality, which made them one of the most reliable names in the days when I bought old wine.

Ahhh, very interesting! Thanks for that info - it definitely held true with this bottle!

posts.cooked:

Thanks for your insights to this question!

Rich_Brown:
Tom_Blach:

Thorin was apparently a very wealthy negociant and consistently used corks of the very highest quality, which made them one of the most reliable names in the days when I bought old wine.

Ahhh, very interesting! Thanks for that info - it definitely held true with this bottle!

I ve had quite a few Burgundies by Thorin from the 60s to 80s that were very nice, .mostly Villages from NSG, Pommard, Chambolle etc., not super complex but very satisfying with age.

posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked: Morgon and MaV are usually the crus that people think of as most ageworthy, but I was at a dinner in 2017 where Xavier Barbet opened a lot of Billards St. Amour. The final flight was 1967, 1969, and 1976, all delicious, and I don;t think anyone would have thought these too old.
